coredata.dc:title: High-performance supercapacitors using carbon dots/titanium dioxide composite electrodes and carbon dot-added sulfuric acid electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Pholauyphon, Wasinee
Bulakhe, Ravindra N.
Manyam, Jedsada
In, Insik
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the electrochemical performance of nanocomposite comprised of TiO2 nanoparticles and sodium polyacrylate-derived carbon dots (CDs) was investigated in H2SO4 electrolyte. The specific capacitance of 5% w/w CDs-containing TiO2 electrode in 1 M H2SO4 demonstrated a significant enhancement of 237%, compared to pristine TiO2. Interestingly, when added to the electrolyte, the specific capacitance was further increased by 292% or as high as 643 mF cm−2. As a result, the addition of CDs in both electrode and electrolyte produced overall improvement of 1,261% through favorably synergistic effects of lessening the series resistance, enhancing surface wettability, diffusion-controlled contribution, and pathways for ion diffusion. These findings are confirmed by thorough electrochemical analysis and data fitting. To the best of our knowledge, this supercapacitor gave the highest areal capacitance value among prior reports of TiO2-based supercapacitors containing H2SO4 electrolytes. Furthermore, it also delivered a high areal energy density of 24.3 μWh cm−2 at a power density of 187.5 μW cm−2 and extraordinary cycling stability of 98.4% capacitance retention after 5,000 cycles. Our work proved that the utilization of CDs as pseudocapacitance promoters and ion facilitators can significantly improve the supercapacitor performance. This strategy will be an innovative way to develop energy storage devices with high performance, excellent stability, simple manufacturing process, and low production cost for practical applications.

head.ce:title: High-performance supercapacitors using carbon dots/titanium dioxide composite electrodes and carbon dot-added sulfuric acid electrolyte

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The H2SO4 system used carbon dots as electrode/electrolyte additives. The charge storage of pristine titanium oxide is dominantly controlled by the surface mechanism. Carbon dots act as pseudocapacitive boosters via improving surface wettability, interaction at the electrode/electrolyte interface, and the diffusion-controlled process. The supercapacitors achieved the highest areal specific capacitance of 643 mF cm-2, representing a total enhancement of 1,261%.

Herein, the electrochemical performance of nanocomposite comprised of TiO2 nanoparticles and sodium polyacrylate-derived carbon dots (CDs) was investigated in H2SO4 electrolyte. The specific capacitance of 5% w/w CDs-containing TiO2 electrode in 1 M H2SO4 demonstrated a significant enhancement of 237%, compared to pristine TiO2. Interestingly, when added to the electrolyte, the specific capacitance was further increased by 292% or as high as 643 mF cm−2. As a result, the addition of CDs in both electrode and electrolyte produced overall improvement of 1,261% through favorably synergistic effects of lessening the series resistance, enhancing surface wettability, diffusion-controlled contribution, and pathways for ion diffusion. These findings are confirmed by thorough electrochemical analysis and data fitting. To the best of our knowledge, this supercapacitor gave the highest areal capacitance value among prior reports of TiO2-based supercapacitors containing H2SO4 electrolytes. Furthermore, it also delivered a high areal energy density of 24.3 μWh cm−2 at a power density of 187.5 μW cm−2 and extraordinary cycling stability of 98.4% capacitance retention after 5,000 cycles. Our work proved that the utilization of CDs as pseudocapacitance promoters and ion facilitators can significantly improve the supercapacitor performance. This strategy will be an innovative way to develop energy storage devices with high performance, excellent stability, simple manufacturing process, and low production cost for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The ever-increasing environmental pollution, global warming, and energy crisis are of a worldwide issue. There is an urgent need for the development of clean, renewable, and highly efficient energy conversion/storage systems [1]. Among several energy storage devices, supercapacitors are ones of the most promising candidates owing to their ability to provide a superior power density, rapid charge/discharge rate, and long cycle stability as compared to batteries and conventional capacitors [2]. Nonetheless, the main drawback of supercapacitors is the lower energy density, compared to batteries. Therefore, the development of materials for producing high-performance supercapacitors with low cost is needed to overcome the existing obstacles. The working principles of supercapacitors can be classified into two charge storage mechanisms. Firstly, electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC), generally using carbonaceous materials as electrodes, store charges via adsorption, desorption, and ion exchange at the electrode/electrolyte interface [3]. Secondly, pseudocapacitors, generally utilizing transition metal oxides and conducting polymers as electrodes, store charges via reversible faradaic reactions at the bulk electrode materials [4]. In general, the pseudocapacitors demonstrate higher specific capacitance and energy density. However, they have lower cycling stability as compared to EDLC [5]. Therefore, combining pseudocapacitors with EDLC materials is a straightforward way to achieve high performance.

Among pseudocapacitor materials, titanium oxide nanoparticles (TiO2) possess pseudocapacitive behaviors and are of abundant availability, excellent chemical stability, nontoxicity, and low production cost [6,7]. In addition, TiO2 has a high dielectric constant and demonstrates faradaic capacitance [8]. Unfortunately, the poor electrical conductivity of the TiO2 resulted in low areal capacitance of TiO2 (90–120 μF cm−2) [9]. As a straightforward solution, combining TiO2 nanoparticles with highly conductive carbon-based nanomaterials is a promising strategy to improve the TiO2-based supercapacitors.

Carbon dots (CDs) are unique and novel carbon-based nanomaterials. They are zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles, which are known as promising materials for supercapacitor applications due to their high chemical stability, ease of production, nanometer size, high electrical conductivity, excellentsurface wettability, various surface functional groups (e.g. carboxyl and amino groups), and low cost [10]. Specifically, the incorporation of CDs in composites was reported to increase conductivity, provide the accessibility of the charged ions through creating pathways and interspaces for ions diffusion, and enhance electron transfer during charge/discharge processes [11–13]. According to the above attributes of TiO2 and CDs, the synergistic strategy of the as-fabricated TiO2/CDs nanocomposites would be intriguing for further studies and developing novel composite electrodes.

In addition to electrodes, electrolytes play important role controlling various electrochemical performances and properties of supercapacitors, such as the power density, rate capability, internal series resistance, cycling stability, and toxicity. This is because electrolytes govern ionic conductivity and also determine the electrochemical potential window of supercapacitors. Furthermore, the interactions between electrode and electrolyte influence the charge storage processes, which are impacted by solvated ion size, ionic conductivity, ionic mobility, and viscosity of electrolytes [14]. Even though the energy density is proportional to the square of the operating voltage window, the use of organic solvents and ionic liquids has to overcome several obstacles [15]. They have the drawbacks of low ionic conductivity, high internal series resistance, high viscosity, electrolyte leakage, expensive solvent, high flammability, and low specific capacitance values [16]. Hence, the aqueous electrolytes are more appropriate than the ionic liquids and organic electrolytes in various aspects [17].

A previous finding revealed that acid electrolytes had low internal resistance and high power capability because of their exceptional ionic conductivity [18]. H2SO4 is a remarkable candidate and widely studied as an acid electrolyte for the fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors due to their extremely high ionic conductivity. For instance, X. B. Liu and coworkers fabricated polyaniline electrode grafted with reduced graphene oxide and tested in 1 M H2SO4. The results showed an outstanding specific capacitance of 1,045 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 [19]. Accordingly, it will be crucially important to further research the impacts of the CDs in acid electrolytes as they are widely popular for uses [20]. In this paper, we thus investigated the CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites as electrodes in H2SO4 electrolyte. Although the use of CDs have been reported for the development of lithium battery and solar cells [21–23], the use of CDs as both simultaneous additives for both electrode and electrolyte are rare. Therefore, it will be a noteworthy discovery on the additive properties of CDs in H2SO4 electrolytes to understand the electrochemical enhancement mechanism introduced by CDs. The supercapacitor performance, rate capability, and cycling stability were evaluated by taking into account the properties of an electrolyte, such as ionic conductivity, ionic mobility, and hydrated ion size. Based on this work, the CDs were demonstrated to be potent supercapacitor performance booster. The developed strategy from this work thus opens new opportunities to achieve high-performance, high-stability, low-cost energy storage devices in the near future.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich unless otherwise noted. Cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1 kDa) was purchased from Spectrum Labs. Conductive carbon paper (0.3 mm thickness, areal resistance < 3 mΩ cm−2) was bought from VECK Store, China. The carbon paper was activated by an electrochemical method in 2 M H2SO4 solution using an Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively, and a constant voltage 2.2 V was applied for 20 min. After activation, the carbon paper was annealed in air at 200 ⁰C for 3 h. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized via the microwave technique whereas CDs were synthesized from sodium polyacrylate via the pyrolysis method as reported in our previous work [24]. Firstly, titanium isopropoxide (14.21 g) and hydrazine hydrate (10.02 g) were combined in DI water with constant stirring for 20 min. After that, the mixedsolution was microwaved at 100 W for 35 min in a microwave oven. The colloidal TiO2 nanoparticles were then filtered out, washed multiple times with ethanol and DIwater, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 4 h, yielding white powder TiO2 nanoparticles.

Secondly, sodium polyacrylate (5 g) was dissolved in1.25 M nitric acid solution (70 mL) in a porcelain crucible and pyrolyzed for 6 hat 250 °C in a furnace. After that, the reaction mixture was combined withDI water and filtered through filter paper. The filtrate was neutralized with a 10 M NaOH solution before being centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The CDs solution was purified by dialyzing for3 days in a dialysis membrane and centrifuging for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. After 1 day of freeze-drying, the brown powder of CDs was collected.

To prepare a working electrode, TiO2 nanoparticles, carbon black, and poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) in a ratio of 80:15:5% w/w were mixed in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (1 mL) solvent and ground until the mixture became a homogenous slurry. Carbon black was added to increase the overall conductivity of the electrode and PVDF was used as a binder. The slurry was coated on a carbon paper (1 cm × 1 cm) and dried in an oven at 80 ⁰C for 12 h to obtain the working electrode (average area and mass loading of active materials are 0.8 cm2 and 0.014 g, respectively). To investigate the effects of CDs on the electrochemical properties, additional electrodes were prepared with two different amounts of CDs (5 and 10% w/w), which are denoted as 5-CDT, and 10-CDT (CDs-added TiO2), respectively. PVDF and carbon black were fixed at 5:15% w/w for all electrodes.

Pure 1 M H2SO4 solutions were used as electrolytes for optimizing the amount of CDs added to the TiO2 electrode. Furthermore, 1 M, 2 M, and 3 M H2SO4 were used to explore the suitable concentration of electrolytes. After that, the best condition of the electrode was used to study the impacts of the CDs in the H2SO4 electrolyte. For the preparation of the CDs-added electrolytes, 2 M H2SO4 solution (70 mL) was mixed with three different amounts of CDs (0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 g) and they were labeled as 0.5-CDH, 1-CDH, and 2-CDH (CDs-added H 2SO4), respectively.

Contact angles were measured using a tensiometer (100 Theta Lite, Biolin Scientific) and 1 µL solution droplets. Electrochemical measurements were performed using a potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT302N, Metrohm). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested in a three-electrode system. The counter and reference electrodes were platinum and Ag/AgCl electrodes, respectively. The CV measurement was carried out using the scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV s−1 whereas the EIS measurement was performed under open-circuit potential with a signal amplitude of 10 mV and frequency from 10-1 to 105 Hz. Galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) analysis was examined using a symmetric two-electrode system using a Swagelok (1 cm diameter) and No. 1 Whatman filter paper as a separator at different currents (0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1, 2 and 3 mA). The areal specific capacitance (mF cm−2) was calculated using Eq. (1).

Fig. 1 a and b indicated that the synthesized TiO2 and CDs are spherical nanoparticles which agree well with the previously reported work [24]. TiO2 and CDs were combined to fabricate the composite electrodes. Three electrodes with different amounts of CDs (0, 5, and 10% w/w) were prepared and denoted as TiO2, 5-CDT, and 10-CDT. The SEM images of 5-CDT and 10-CDT depicted that the addition of CDs led to more agglomerated morphology (Fig. 1c and d). The maximum amount of the CDs had to be kept at 10% w/w because the dissolution of CDs in aqueous electrolytes was observed when 15% w/w. CDs were used in the composite electrodes due to their high aqueous solubility. The fabricated CDs/TiO2 composite electrodes were tested in 1 M H2SO4 solution as electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs in TiO2 electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The working electrodes were prepared using the carbon paper as the current collector. Prior to deposition of electrode materials, the carbon paper was activated by using electrochemical activation in acid solution to produce surface functional groups and defects on carbon paper for better adsorption of materials [25]. First of all, the electrochemical properties of the pristine TiO2, 5-CDT, and 10-CDT electrodes were evaluated by CV, GCD, and EIS methods in a three-electrode system containing 1 M H2SO4 as an aqueous electrolyte. Fig. 2 a illustrated the CV profiles of carbon paper, the pristine TiO2, 5-CDT, and 10-CDT samples at the same scan rate and a voltage window from 0 to 0.8 V. In the absence of active materials, the carbon paper electrode as a control provided a low capacitance of 0.3 mF cm−2 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The CV profiles of all electrodes with active materials exhibit quasi-rectangular shapes, indicating pseudocapacitive characteristics, which differ from the redox peaks observed in the KOH electrolyte [24]. Therefore, type of electrolyte is one of the key factors controlling the mechanism of charge storage in supercapacitor. As shown in Table 1 , it can be noted that 5-CDT (121 mF cm−2) electrode had a higher areal capacitance than pristine TiO2 (51 mF cm−2) by 237%. As the same electrode was used, the extent of enhancement in H2SO4 system is higher than that of the KOH system (158% increase in specific capacitance) [24]. A further addition of CDs to 10% w/w caused a reduction in capacitance. This is probably because the CDs agglomerated and phase-separated from TiO2 in the composite. As equally important as electrode composition, the electrolyte concentration is controlling the electrochemical properties of supercapacitor [14]. To evaluate the effects of H2SO4 concentration on the electrochemical behaviors of 5-CDT electrode, the CV profiles of 1, 2, and 3 M H2SO4 solutions were demonstrated in Fig. 2b. The CV loop area of 2 M H2SO4 is markedly larger than those of other concentrations, meaning that it possessed the highest areal specific capacitance (220 mF cm−2) as shown in Table 1. An increase in concentration from 1 M to 2 M increased the ionic conductivity, leading to enhance the specific capacitance. In contrast, a further increase above 2 M caused high electrolyte viscosity, which reduced the ionic mobility and the specific capacitance [26]. Fig. 2c exhibits the CV profiles of the 5-CDT electrode measured with different scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1 in 2 M H2SO4 electrolyte. The specific capacitances calculated from the CV profiles are 220, 197, 194, 184, and 166 mF cm−2 as scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV s−1. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, the specific capacitance is the highest at the lowest scan rate owing to their sufficient time for ion diffusion into the inner surface of the electrode for efficient charge storage. On the contrary, a further increase in scan rate causes a limited charge storage at the outer surface of the electrode, signifying a reduction in the specific capacitance for all electrodes [27]. Galvanic charge–discharge curves of 5-CDT at difference current rates are displayed in Fig. 2e. The specific capacitances of 5-CDT electrode are 262, 244, 240, 232, 228, 210, and 197 mF cm−2 at the currents of 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1, 2, and 3 mA, respectively, equivalent to an impressive rate capability of 75.2%. The EIS was performed to examine inner resistance and electron transfer/ion diffusion process. The Nyquist plots of pristine TiO2, 5-CDT, and 10-CDT electrode containing 1 M H2SO4 as an electrolyte were exhibited in Fig. 2f. The curves of the CDs/TiO2 composite electrodes are more vertical than that of the pristine TiO2 over low-frequency regions, representing that the CDs composite reduced the diffusion resistance in the H2SO4 electrolyte. Especially, the 5-CDT electrode demonstrated the most vertical line which agrees with the highest specific capacitance. Additionally, Fig. S1a demonstrated bode plots of all electrodes, indicating that phase angles at y-intercept of the TiO2, 5-CDT, and 10-CDT samples are −64.3°, −76.5°, and −73.7°, respectively. The 5-CDT exhibited a value of phase angle closest to −90°, suggesting the highest electrochemical capacitance [28]. Hence, these results prove that the presence of CDs improved electrochemical performance of TiO2 electrode by reducing the resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs in H2SO4 electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: Based on our previous work, the fabrication of supercapacitor using CDs/TiO2 composite electrodes and KOH electrolytes significantly improved the supercapacitor performance. Interestingly, the introduction of CDs in the TiO2 electrode enhanced the pseudocapacitive contribution and the specific capacitance [24]. Despite employing H2SO4 instead of KOH electrolyte, the addition of CDs into the TiO2 electrode greatly improved the specific capacitance as mentioned above. To further examine the impacts of CDs in acid electrolytes, the CDs were added in 2 M H2SO4 electrolytes using different amounts of CDs (0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 g denoted as 1-CDH, 0.5-CDH, and 2-CDH) and the 5-CDT electrode based on the above results. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the CV profiles of 5-CDT in all CDs/H2SO4 electrolyte systems presented quasi-rectangular shapes, confirming pseudocapacitive characteristics [29]. The 1-CDH electrolyte provided the largest CV loop area, demonstrating the highest areal specific capacitance of 643 mF cm−2 (consistent to 45.9 F g−1), while H2SO4, 0.5-CDH, and 2-CDH reveal the areal specific capacitance of 220, 438, and 368 mF cm−2, respectively, at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. As compared to pure H2SO4, the 1-CDH electrolyte greatly boosted the areal specific capacitance by 292%. This improvement is again higher than the KOH system [24]. Nevertheless, when the amount of CDs was above 0.02 g, the agglomeration of CDs had an adverse impact, resulting in an apparent decrease of specific capacitance [30]. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the CV profiles of 5-CDT in 1-CDH still retained the quasi-rectangular shape at scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1, signifying the pseudocapacitive behavior. The areal specific capacitances are 643, 567, 534, 493, and 481 mF cm−2 at a scan rate of 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV s−1, respectively. Fig. 3c indicated the GCD curves as a function of current, which displayed the quasi-linear shapes. This implies the pseudocapacitive characteristics [29], consistent with the CV analysis. The areal specific capacitances of 5-CDT in 1-CDH equal 706, 672, 654, 639, 561, 548, and 512 mF cm−2 at 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1, 2, and 3 mA, respectively, unveiling an outstanding rate capability of 72.5% even when the current density was increased five times. The acid electrolyte (H2SO4) remarkably exhibited better rate capability than that of alkaline electrolyte (KOH), revealing one of the vital benefits of H2SO4 electrolyte. Fig. 3d show the Nyquist plots of pure H2SO4, 0.5-CDH, 1-CDH, and 2-CDH. These EIS curves did not illustrate semicircles at the low-frequency region but revealed the nearly vertical lines for all electrolyte systems, which indicates low series resistance and high ionic conductivity. Furthermore, the Bode plots (Fig. S1b) illustrate that the phase angle at y-intercepts of the 1-CDH system is −76.8°, which is the closest to −90° in comparison to pure H2SO4 (-69.3°), 0.5-CDH (-74.1°), and 2-CDH (-70.9°) [28]. These results therefore confirm that 5-CDT in 1-CDH exhibited the lowest series resistance, leading to high ionic mobility and electrolyte transport capability [31]. These results are also consistent with the highest specific capacitance.

Based on the above results, the presence of CDs in electrodes and electrolytes greatly boosted the specific capacitance and reduced series resistance. As described in previous publications [32–34], the CDs can improve the surface wettability between electrode and electrolyte and provide unique electroactive properties and pathways for ion transport, which enhance the electron transfer process. Hence, the electrochemical analysis of the charge transfer process was implemented to further justify the impacts of CDs on the TiO2 supercapacitor performance in H2SO4 electrolytes as follows.

Several electrochemical characteristics can be distinguished from the CV behavior as a function of scan rate. The charge storage process can be classified into two types. The first is surface-controlled process (EDLC and near-surface redox reactions) and the second is diffusion-controlled or intercalation processes (ion insertion/extrusion in bulk materials) [35]. The relationship between current response (i) and scan rate (v) was studied based on the Cottrell’s equation (Eq. (4)) [36].
(4)

Eq. (4) was rearranged as
i = a
v

and then
i = b l o g v + l o g a
, where a and b are variable parameters [37]. From the linear plot between log i vs log v (Fig. 4 a and d), the slope is the b value. The b value designates the charge storage mechanisms. They are diffusion-controlled or surface-controlled mechanism when the b value equals 0.5 or 1, respectively [38,39]. Notably, all electrodes and electrolytes unveiled the b values closer to 1 than 0.5, pointing towards a more dominant surface-controlled contribution (Table 2). Based on the Cottrell’s method, the addition of CDs to electrodes and electrolytes led to increase more contribution from the diffusion-controlled process. The CDs acted as pseudocapacitance promoters which gave rise to a better ion diffusion into the inner of electrode materials, which caused a reduced series resistance and improved specific capacitance as illustrated in the above results.

To further analyze the electron transfer process, the linear plots between the peak current (ip ) and the scan rate (v) (Fig. 4b and e) was constructed to assess the adsorption-controlled process, based on Eq. (5) [40].

where n is the number of transferred electrons (mol), F is the Faraday’s constant (96,485 C mol−1), A is the electroactive area (cm2), T is the temperature (298 K), R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), and Γ refers to the surface coverage of the adsorbed species (mol cm−2). In comparison, the correlation between peak current (ip ) and square root of scan rates (v 1/2) was performed (Fig. 4c and f) to represent the diffusion-controlled process following the Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (6)) [41].

where C0 refers to the bulk concentration of the electroactive species (mol cm−3) and Do refers to the diffusion coefficient of the electroactive species (cm2 s−1). Based on these plots, the coefficients of determination (R2) were obtained (Table 2), demonstrating the electron transfer mechanism. From Fig. 4b and e, the R2 values are significantly higher than those in Fig. 4c and f. Hence, the charge storage mechanism of CDs-added supercapacitors is predominantly the surface-controlled process. Importantly, the presence of CDs in TiO2 supercapacitors enhanced the contribution from the diffusion-controlled process as confirmed by both Cottrell’s and adsorption/desorption analyses. An enhancement of the diffusion process led to the improvement in specific capacitance as more electrolytic ions diffused deeply into the inner materials.

According to our previous work [24], the analysis of the scan rate effects is performed. In KOH electrolytes, the charge storage mechanism is dominantly controlled by the diffusion process that contrasts with H2SO4 electrolytes. Hence, these results show that the type of electrolytes is a vital parameter governing the charge storage mechanism and ion transport in supercapacitors. The specific capacitance in the KOH system is much higher than that in the H2SO4 system owing to their predominant diffusion-controlled process. On the other hand, the H2SO4 system gave much higher rate capability than the KOH system and larger enhancement when the CDs were added, consistent with the previous work [42].

As verified using the Cottrell’s and Randles-Sevcik’s methods above, the charge storage mechanism is controlled by a dominant surface capacitive contribution and the CDs promoted the diffusion process. The diffusion-controlled and surfaced-controlled contributions are further investigated using the Trasatti’s method to quantify the percentage of charge storage contributions [43]. The total capacitance (CT ) is the sum between the diffusion-controlled capacitance (Ci ) and surface-controlled capacitance (Co ) as illustrated in the Eq. (7) [44],

In contrast, assuming scan rate being zero (v = 0), the electrolyte ions have infinite time to diffuse into the electrode, and therefore, the total capacitance can be construed from the y-intercept of linear plot (Fig. 5b) as stated in Eq. To additionally examine the charge storage and ion transport process, the Dunn’s method was used. The current responses at a fixed potential i(v) are a combination of surface-controlled (k1 ) and diffusion-controlled (k2 ) processes, following Eq. (10) [43,46].

As depicted in Fig. 5c and Table 3, with the addition of CDs into electrode and electrolyte, the surface-controlled process is a dominant one and the use of CDs improved the diffusion process, consistent with the analysis using the Trasatti’s method. This analysis exhibited that the CDs influenced the charge storage behavior and ion transport between electrode and electrolyte by enhancing the diffusion-controlled contribution. To further examine the effects of scan rate, Fig. 5d depicts that the specific capacitance reduced with increasing scan rates due to the restricted time, causing a limitation of the rate diffusion process. This phenomenon agrees with the decrease in the diffusion-controlled process when increasing the scan rate from 5 to 100 mV s−1. Fig. 5e and f further proved that the additions of CDs in electrode and electrolyte significantly enlarged the proportion of capacitive contribution from the diffusion-controlled process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle measurement and mechanistic discussion
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above-mentioned analysis, the presence ofCDs in the electrode and electrolyte enhanced the diffusion-controlled mechanism. To further explore the effects of CDs, H2SO4 solution was used to measure the contact angles on TiO2, 5-CDT, and 10-CDT (Fig. 6 a). The 5-CDT produced the smallest initial contact angle of 22°, followed by 10-CDT (24°) and TiO2 (34°). These results confirmed that CDs enhanced hydrophilicity and interactions between electrode and electrolyte, resulting in improved diffusion of electrolyte ions. As the result of agglomeration and phase separation between the CDs and TiO2 in 10-CDT, the contact angle of 10-CDT is slightly bigger than that of 5-CDT. The H2SO4 droplet was absorbed completely in the 5-CDT and 10-CDT electrodes after 2 s, hence, confirming that adding the CDs to the electrode improved the surface wettability and hydrophilicity of electrode surface. The contact angles agreed well with the capacitance values, indicating that the wettability and supercapacitor performance are strongly related. Next, the effects of CDs in electrolytes were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6b, the contact angles of 0.5-CDH (19°), 1-CDH (17°), and 2-CDH (19°) droplets on the 5-CDT electrode surface were obtained. The contact angles are again consistent with the specific capacitances. The addition of optimum amount of CDs in the H2SO4 electrolyte (1-CDH) enhanced surface wettability between the electrode/electrolyte interface. The 1-CDH droplet was completely absorbed on the electrode surface after only 1 s. This study proved that the amount of added CDs in the electrode and electrolyte influenced the hydrophilicity and wettability at the electrode/electrolyte interface, which controlled the specific capacitance values.

According to electrochemical analysis and contact angle measurement, enhanced interaction between electrode and electrolyte occurred via increasing diffusion-controlled process and surface wettability, which synergistically boosted the supercapacitor performance. First of all, the enhanced wettability provided by the CDs led to better electrical contact between electrode and electrolyte and permeability of ionic species into the bulk electrode (Fig. 7 a), as confirmed by the contact angle results. Moreover,the carboxyl groups of the CDs could play a role as interfacial linkers to the TiO2 nanoparticle surface, giving pathways for ion transport during the charge/discharge mechanisms, as proved by the increased diffusion-controlled process [47,48]. The interactions between TiO2 and CDs were further investigated using the FT-IR technique. For the 5-CDT composite sample, the titanium carboxylate stretching (R-(O=)COTi) and TiOC stretching were observed at 1520 and 1095 cm−1, respectively, which were not found from the pure TiO2 and CDs samples (Fig. S2). The appearance of these new peaks suggest the interactions between TiO2 and CDs. Moreover, since the CDs contained oxygen functional groups, they can act pseudocapacitive sites providing Faradaic reactions and enhancing the capacitance [49,50]. Lastly, the presence of CDs in the electrolyte also worked as charge-transport facilitators, boosting the ionic conductivity of the electrolytes [51]. All of the synergistic impacts offered by the CDs resulted in 1,261% or more than a twelve-fold increase in the specific capacitance value.

Comparing this and previous research [24], the type of electrolyte is another key influence on the supercapacitor performance. The energy storage process in the supercapacitor made with TiO2 electrode containing H2SO4 electrolyte is contributed from the predominantadsorption mechanism (EDLC behavior). In contrast, the TiO2 electrode in the KOH electrolytedisplayed a prominent diffusion-controlled contribution (pseudocapacitive behavior) [24]. The differences in capacitance values and charge storage process between H2SO4 and KOH can be attributed to the impacts of anions and cations electrolyte. The rates of electrolyte ion permeability into the active materials differ due to their hydrated ionic radius and ionic mobility (Fig. 7b) [52]. Despite the fact that K+ ions have slightly larger hydrated diameters than H+ ions, the capacitance value in KOH is greater than that in the H2SO4 electrolyte. This is due to SO4 2- anions having a much larger hydrated ionic radius than OH– anions [53]. Based on the previous work [53], the effects of anion types influence on the supercapacitor performance were examined. As the hydrated ionic spheres increased: OH– < SO4 2-, the specific capacitance value decreased. Therefore, the size of the hydrated anion is thus significantly crucial to specific capacitance values. In comparison to this work (H2SO4 electrolyte), a higher contribution of diffusion-controlled mechanism and specific capacitance values in the KOH system can be attributed to the small hydrated radii of both anions and cations with high ionic mobility, leading to more ion diffusion into the active matrix. These findings reveal that the electrolyte is a deciding factor on the charge transport/storage mechanism and ultimately governing the electrochemical performance. Since the CDs enhanced the charge storage contribution from the diffusion process and the H2SO4 system is predominantly surface-controlled, the pseudocapacitive enhancement by the CDs is far greater in the H2SO4 system than the KOH system. These principal insights will be advantageous in the future development of supercapacitors.

Excellent long-term cycle stability is another key parameter for supercapacitor applications. Fig. 8 a exhibited the cycling stability of the 5-CDT electrode in 2 M H2SO4 and 1-CDH electrolytes, indicating exceptional areal capacitance retention after 5,000 cycles of 90.9% and 98.4%, respectively. Both electrolyte systems expressed remarkable stability towards numerous charge/discharge cycles. Interestingly, 1-CDH delivered higher cycling stability than that of pure H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Nyquist plots of the 5-CDT electrode in 2 M H2SO4 and 1-CDH electrolytes after 5,000 charge/discharge cycles are shown in Fig. 8b. The vertical lines of 5-CDT in 2 M H2SO4 and 1-CDH solution displayed the reduction of the slope in the low-frequency region after taking the cycle stability test, indicating lower capacitive properties. Furthermore, the intercept at the x-axis denotes the series resistance of the system as exhibited in the inset Fig. 8b. These signified that the series resistance of pure H2SO4 system emphatically increased after 5,000 cycles (from 1.74 to 1.98 Ω), whereas series resistance of 1-CDH system only slightly changed after the 1st and 5,000th cycles from 1.22 to 1.27 Ω. The Bode plots (Fig. 8c) presented phase angles at y-intercepts of 5-CDT in 2 M H2SO4 (-69.3° and −67.2°) and 1-CDH (-75.6° and −74.5°) electrolytes before and after 5,000 cycles. The 5-CDT in 1-CDH electrolyte demonstrated a lower reduction in phase angles, comparing to that in the pure H2SO4 electrolyte. Thus, these results confirmed that the CDs helped reduce the series resistance, resulting in an enhancement in cycle stability. In comparison, the CDs/KOH electrolyte indicated 91.4% of initial capacitance value after 5,000 cycles in our previous report [24], but the CDs/H2SO4 electrolyte provided better capacitance retention of 98.4%. The insertion/de-insertion of large size cationic ions (K+ > H+) during the charge/discharge process might cause significant compositional damage to the electrode [14]. Despite higher capacitance value in the KOH electrolyte, the H2SO4 electrolyte also has a combination of high capacitance, rate capability, and cycling stability. Therefore, the H2SO4 electrolyte is an exceptional alternative for developing high-performance, high-stability supercapacitors.

Table 4 displayed the TiO2 composite supercapacitors using H2SO4 as an electrolyte. Our supercapacitor exhibited the areal specific capacitance of 643 mF cm−2, which is not only the best but also demonstrates excellent cycling stability. Furthermore, our synthesis method of TiO2 nanoparticles is much easier to fabricate than the TiO2 nanotube arrays in previous reports [54–59]. Energy density and power density are also two crucial parameters of the energy storage performance. Fig. 8d demonstrated the Ragone plots of the 5-CDT in H2SO4 and 1-CDH electrolytes, presenting the correlation between areal energy density and areal power density. The 1-CDH system delivered a high areal energy density of 24.3 μWh cm−2 and a power density of 187.5 μW cm−2, which are relatively comparable to the battery counterparts. More crucially, this work evinced that our CDs indicate versatility as exceptionally additives, resulting in practical, high-performance, extremely stable, low-cost supercapacitors. These findings point to the supercapacitor with high performance and extraordinary stability. This strategy is feasible to employ in other electrode and electrolyte systems, which opens a new opportunity to further developments.

In this work, we focused on developing high-performance supercapacitors and investigating factors that influenced electrochemical properties and supercapacitor performance. The CDs acted as the pseudocapacitive boosters and ion facilitators when used as electrode and electrolyte additives. As compared to pristine TiO2 supercapacitor using H2SO4 electrolyte, the CDs are responsible for an extraordinary 1,261% increase in specific capacitance. The electrochemical and mechanistic analyses indicated that the utmost impact on the supercapacitor performance was related to the introduction of CDs and type of electrolytes. Firstly, the functions of CDs comprised of the ability to improve surface wettability for generating an advantageous interface of electrodes/electrolytes and electrical contact, ion transport to inner bulk electrode materials, as well as the increase in the diffusion-controlled contribution, and reduction in series resistance. Secondly, the type of electrolytes is another critical factor that influences electrochemical performance and charges storage behaviors. The introduction of CDs in both electrode and electrolyte demonstrated the synergistic functions causing the remarkable enhancement of electrochemical performance. This work indicated that our synthesized CDs are versatile additives that can improve the specific capacitance in H2SO4 systems. This breakthrough will potentially aid in the development of high-performance, low-cost, and long-lasting energy storage systems.
